title: What Is Sustainable Architecture and Why It Matters in Indian Cities
author: CITRA Associates
metaDescription: Understand what sustainable architecture really means in Indian cities — passive design, material choices, water management, and why it matters for your next project.
excerpt: Sustainable architecture in India goes beyond solar panels and certifications. This article explains passive design principles, embodied carbon, water management strategies, and why the stakes of getting sustainability right have never been higher for India's rapidly urbanising cities.

faqs.question: Does sustainable architecture cost more to build in India?
faqs.answer: Passive sustainable strategies like orientation, window placement, and shading add minimal upfront cost while reducing lifetime energy bills significantly. Active systems like solar panels have higher upfront costs but strong payback periods, typically 5 to 8 years in most Indian cities. The total cost of ownership of a sustainably designed home is almost always lower than a conventional equivalent.

faqs.question: What is GRIHA and LEED certification for buildings in India?
faqs.answer: GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment) is India's national green building rating system developed by TERI, while LEED is an internationally recognised standard. Both evaluate buildings on energy performance, water efficiency, material use, and indoor environmental quality. GRIHA is specifically calibrated for Indian climate zones and construction practices.

faqs.question: Can a small residential home in India be designed sustainably?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Sustainable design principles apply at every scale. For a modest residential home, passive strategies — correct orientation, cross-ventilation, shading, and thermal mass — are the most impactful and cost-effective interventions. A well-designed 1,200 sq ft home can outperform a poorly designed 3,000 sq ft one in both comfort and energy use.
